_comment: Two newsletters that take opposite routes through the same manifest. The first matches two interests and escalates to the Inbox; the second matches none, clears confidence_threshold, and files itself as a note without ever being seen. Same capability, same action-item type, different policy outcome. Used by `samaritan seed` and by `pnpm run-capability newsletter-digest --input-file capabilities/newsletter-digest/fixtures/demo.json`.

email.message.subject: The agent evals gap nobody wants to own
email.message.body:
Everyone shipping agent products has the same unspoken problem. The evals that got you to a demo are not the evals that keep you honest in production, and almost nobody has budget for the second kind.

The teams doing this well have stopped treating evals as a test suite and started treating them as a product surface. They version them. They review regressions in standup. One team we spoke to runs a weekly retrieval bake-off against last quarter's queries and treats a drop as a Sev-2.

What is striking is how much of this is product management work wearing an engineering hat: deciding what good looks like, defending the definition when it is inconvenient, and keeping the scoreboard visible.

Read the full teardown: https://latent.space/p/agent-evals-gap
The bake-off template: https://latent.space/p/retrieval-bakeoff

email.message.subject: Coffee futures, a very good dog, and the return of the mall
email.message.body:
Good morning. Coffee futures hit a nine-month high, which means your cold brew habit is about to get more expensive and your barista is about to get more philosophical about it.

In other news, a golden retriever in Ohio has been elected honorary mayor for the third consecutive term, running unopposed on a platform of enthusiasm.

Retail is quietly having a moment again. Foot traffic at suburban malls is up year over year for the first time since 2019, and the anchor tenants are no longer department stores.

More: https://morningbrew.com/daily/2026/07/20
